topic: The Changing Landscapes of the UK
subject: geography

items.q: What is a positive climate feedback loop?
items.correct: A process where warming triggers changes that cause more warming — e.g. melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, which absorbs more heat, melting more ice (ice-albedo feedback).
items.misconception: Positive feedback means a good outcome for the climate.
items.random_wrong: Positive feedback means the climate balances itself out.
items.why: 'Positive' here means amplifying, not beneficial. Negative feedbacks (which stabilise) are positive for us. The terminology trips students up — it refers to direction of change, not desirability.

items.correct: Longshore drift carries sediment past a change in coastline direction (e.g. river mouth); deposition continues into deeper water, building a curved depositional landform with a hook.
items.misconception: A spit forms where the river deposits its sediment at the coast.
items.random_wrong: A spit is a small island just off the coast.
items.why: Spits are coastal (longshore drift) deposits, not river deposits. The hook forms where wind shifts the current. Students confuse spits with deltas — different processes, different settings.

items.q: Why does urbanisation produce a flashier hydrograph?
items.correct: Impermeable surfaces (concrete, tarmac) reduce infiltration, drains speed up runoff to rivers, shortening lag time and increasing peak discharge — raising flood risk downstream.
items.misconception: Urbanisation reduces flood risk because drains take water away safely.
items.random_wrong: Urbanisation makes hydrographs flatter because cities use a lot of water.
items.why: Drains accelerate water to the river rather than removing it — they shift the peak earlier and higher. Urban basins respond faster, giving less warning and higher peak floods.

items.q: Which line divides upland and lowland Britain?
items.why: It's Tees-Exe Line. “Watford Line” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Where are softer sedimentary rocks found in UK?
items.why: Correct answer: Southeast England. “Scottish Highlands” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many km of coastline does the UK have?
items.why: Exact answer: 17820 km. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
